message:
feat(web/lessons): add 15 new lesson components and overhaul existing lesson content

New lesson components (15 total):
- acfs-doctor-lesson.tsx: ACFS doctor diagnostic tool walkthrough
- agents-md-lesson.tsx: AGENTS.md configuration and multi-agent coordination
- ast-grep-lesson.tsx: AST-based code search and transformation patterns
- bv-lesson.tsx: Beads Viewer (bv) for read-only repo inspection
- caam-lesson.tsx: Claude Agent Activity Monitor usage guide
- ci-cd-lesson.tsx: CI/CD pipeline setup and GitHub Actions workflows
- cloud-infra-lesson.tsx: Cloud infrastructure management patterns
- context-mastery-lesson.tsx: LLM context window optimization techniques
- debugging-agents-lesson.tsx: Debugging AI coding agents effectively
- lang-runtimes-lesson.tsx: Language runtime management (Rust, Node, Python)
- modern-cli-lesson.tsx: Modern CLI tool patterns and best practices
- project-bootstrap-lesson.tsx: Project scaffolding with acfs-newproj
- security-layers-lesson.tsx: Security layers in the ACFS stack
- swarm-coordination-lesson.tsx: Multi-agent swarm coordination patterns
- tailscale-lesson.tsx: Tailscale VPN setup and mesh networking

Existing lesson improvements (50+ files):
- Enrich all existing lessons with expanded content, better code examples,
  interactive exercises, and consistent section structure using the shared
  lesson-components library (Section, KeyConcept, CommandList, etc.)
- lesson-components.tsx: add useRef-based timer cleanup for copy feedback,
  replace raw navigator.clipboard with copyTextToClipboard utility for
  broader browser compatibility
- index.tsx: register all 15 new lesson components in the render switch
- lessons.ts: extend lesson metadata with new slugs and categories
- lessonProgress.ts: improve progress tracking with better persistence

Learn pages:
- lesson-content.tsx, page.tsx: update dynamic lesson routing
- commands/: enhance command reference with manifest-sourced data
- tools/[tool]/: improve tool detail pages with richer content

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

} + delay={0.1} + > + + AI agents depend on correctly installed tools. A broken{" "} + PATH, missing binary, or stale config can waste + hours of debugging time. ACFS includes three maintenance systems that + catch problems early, before they derail your work. + + +

+ + + + {/* Section 2: acfs doctor */} +
} + delay={0.15} + > + + The acfs doctor command runs health checks on + every component installed by ACFS. It checks binary existence, version + constraints, and configuration validity — giving you a quick snapshot + of your entire environment. + + +
+ +
+ +
+ + Run acfs doctor at the start + of every session. It takes under 5 seconds and catches issues before + they waste hours of agent time. + +
+
+ + + + {/* Section 3: Nightly Auto-Updates */} +
} + delay={0.2} + > + + A systemd timer runs updates automatically every night. It updates tool + binaries, pulls latest configs, and runs doctor afterward to verify + everything is still healthy. + + +
+ +
+ +
+ + Don't disable nightly updates unless you have a specific reason. + Tool version drift between agents causes subtle, hard-to-debug + failures. + +
+
+ + + + {/* Section 4: Workspace Setup */} +
} + delay={0.25} + > + + ACFS creates a ready-to-use workspace with a tmux session and project + folder structure. Everything is configured so you can SSH in and + immediately start working. + + +
+ +

+ + The workspace is designed so you can SSH in, run{" "} + tmux attach, and immediately + start working. Everything persists across disconnections. + +
+
+ + + + {/* Section 5: SRPS: Resource Protection */} +
} + delay={0.3} + > + + The System Resource Protection Service prevents + agents from overwhelming the VPS. It monitors CPU, memory, disk, and + process count, taking automatic action when thresholds are exceeded. + + +
+ 90% for > 60 seconds → throttle agents +# - Memory usage > 85% → warn, > 95% → emergency cleanup +# - Disk usage > 90% → block new writes +# - Process count > 500 → kill orphaned processes + +# View SRPS alerts +srps alerts --last 10 + +# Configure thresholds +srps config set cpu-warning 80 +srps config set memory-critical 95 + +# When SRPS triggers: +# 1. Logs the event +# 2. Sends Agent Mail notification to all agents +# 3. Pauses lowest-priority agent work +# 4. Waits for resources to recover +# 5. Resumes work automatically`} + showLineNumbers + /> +
+ +
+ + SRPS works with SBH (Storage Ballast Helper) + which pre-allocates disk space as an emergency buffer. When disk + fills up, SBH releases the ballast so the system can recover + gracefully. + +

@@ -0,0 +1,273 @@ +"use client"; + +import { + Search, + Code, + RefreshCw, + Zap, + Shield, + FileText, +} from "lucide-react"; +import { + Section, + Paragraph, + CodeBlock, + TipBox, + Highlight, + Divider, + GoalBanner, + FeatureCard, + FeatureGrid, +} from "./lesson-components"; + +export function AstGrepLesson() { + return ( +
+ + Master structural code search with ast-grep — find patterns by AST + shape, not string matching, for refactors, policy checks, and code + analysis. + + +
} + delay={0.1} + > + + Traditional regex-based grep searches treat + code as flat text. That means matches inside comments, strings, and + unrelated contexts all show up as false positives. Renaming a function + with sed can silently break imports, type annotations, and + documentation in one pass. + + + ast-grep takes a different approach: it parses + your code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) and matches against the + structural shape of the code. A pattern like{" "} + useState($INIT) only matches actual function + calls — never a comment that mentions useState or a string containing + the word. + + +
+ + } + title="Refactoring" + description="Rename APIs and change import forms with structural precision" + gradient="from-primary/20 to-violet-500/20" + /> + } + title="Policy Enforcement" + description="Enforce coding patterns consistently across repositories" + gradient="from-amber-500/20 to-orange-500/20" + /> + } + title="Bug Detection" + description="DCG and UBS use it internally for command and bug analysis" + gradient="from-red-500/20 to-rose-500/20" + /> + } + title="Language-Aware" + description="Understands 20+ languages including TypeScript, Rust, Python, and Go" + gradient="from-emerald-500/20 to-teal-500/20" + /> + +
+
+ + + +
} + delay={0.15} + > + + ast-grep patterns use $NAME to capture a single + AST node and $$$ARGS to capture zero or more + nodes (variadic). The rest of the pattern is matched literally against + the code structure. + + +
+ +
+ + + $NAME matches a single AST node (one + expression, one identifier). $$$ARGS matches + zero or more nodes — think of it like a variadic or rest parameter. + +
+ + + +
} + delay={0.2} + > + + ast-grep can do structural rewrites using the{" "} + -r flag. Captured metavariables from the + pattern are available in the replacement, so transformations preserve + the original structure. + + +
+ +
+ + + Always use --interactive or review the diff + before applying rewrites to a whole codebase. + +
+ + + +
} + delay={0.25} + > + + The power combo: use rg for speed to narrow + candidate files, then pipe into ast-grep for + structural precision. ripgrep scans millions of lines per second; + ast-grep ensures only real matches survive. + + +
+ +
+
+ + + +
} + delay={0.3} + > + + DCG uses ast-grep's Rust crates internally + to parse commands and match destructive patterns at the AST level.{" "} + UBS uses AST helpers for cross-language bug + detection, including resource lifecycle analysis and type narrowing + checks. + + +
+ +
+ + + ast-grep's structural matching is why DCG has zero false positives + on command detection — it understands the actual shape of commands, not + just string patterns. + +
+ + + +
} + delay={0.35} + > + + For reusable checks, define rules in YAML files and run them with{" "} + ast-grep scan. A{" "} + sgconfig.yml at your project root tells + ast-grep where to find rule definitions. + + +
+ +
+
+
+ ); +}

+// =============================================================================
+// HIGHLIGHTED SNIPPET - renders text with highlighted search matches
+// =============================================================================
+function HighlightedSnippet({
+  text,
+  highlights,
+}: {
+  text: string;
+  highlights: string[];
+}) {
+  if (highlights.length === 0) {
+    return <span>{text}</span>;
+  }
+
+  const escapedHighlights = highlights.map((h) =>
+    h.replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g, "\\$&")
+  );
+  const regex = new RegExp(`(${escapedHighlights.join("|")})`, "gi");
+  const parts = text.split(regex);
+
+  return (
+    <span>
+      {parts.map((part, i) => {
+        const isHighlight = highlights.some(
+          (h) => h.toLowerCase() === part.toLowerCase()
+        );
+        return isHighlight ? (
+          <span
+            key={i}
+            className="text-primary font-semibold bg-primary/15 px-0.5 rounded"
+          >
+            {part}
+          </span>
+        ) : (
+          <span key={i}>{part}</span>
+        );
+      })}
+    </span>
+  );
+}
+
+// =============================================================================
+// KNOWLEDGE GRAPH SVG - renders connection lines between nodes
+// =============================================================================
+function KnowledgeGraphSVG({
+  nodes,
+}: {
+  nodes: KnowledgeNode[];
+}) {
+  // Precompute unique edges so each pair draws only one line
+  const edges: [number, number][] = [];
+  const edgeSet = new Set<string>();
+  nodes.forEach((node, i) => {
+    node.connections.forEach((j) => {
+      if (j < nodes.length) {
+        const key = `${Math.min(i, j)}-${Math.max(i, j)}`;
+        if (!edgeSet.has(key)) {
+          edgeSet.add(key);
+          edges.push([i, j]);
+        }
+      }
+    });
+  });
+
+  return (
+    <div className="absolute inset-0 pointer-events-none">
+      <svg
+        width="100%"
+        height="100%"
+        className="absolute inset-0"
+        viewBox="0 0 100 100"
+        preserveAspectRatio="none"
+      >
+        {edges.map(([from, to]) => {
+          const totalNodes = nodes.length;
+          const x1 = ((from + 0.5) / totalNodes) * 100;
+          const x2 = ((to + 0.5) / totalNodes) * 100;
+          return (
+            <motion.line
+              key={`${from}-${to}`}
+              x1={`${x1}%`}
+              y1="30%"
+              x2={`${x2}%`}
+              y2="70%"
+              stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.06)"
+              strokeWidth="0.5"
+              initial={{ pathLength: 0 }}
+              animate={{ pathLength: 1 }}
+              transition={{ duration: 0.8, delay: 0.3 }}
+            />
+          );
+        })}
+      </svg>
+    </div>
+  );
+}
